chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: CSCE-726
Course Title: Large-scale Optimization for Machine Learning
Section: Spring 2026
Course Website: CANVAS
Time: Monday, Wednesday, Friday (3:00-3:50pm)
Location: H.R. Bright Building, Room 126
Credit Hours: 3

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Large-scale optimization plays an important role in machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence (AI). In the past twenty years, there are many efficient algorithms that have been proposed and employed for solving various machine learning problems, e.g., stochastic gradient descent (SGD), adaptive gradient methods (e.g., AdaGrad, Adam), momentum-based methods, etc. Many of these algorithms have been implemented in modern deep learning packages such as Pytorch and Tensorflow.

This course will cover fundamental optimization algorithms and their analysis for solving advanced machine learning problems. It will cover advanced machine learning methods and classic and modern stochastic gradient-based algorithms. Students will not only need to understand the fundamental principle of machine learning and convergence analysis of various optimization algorithms but also need to implement them through Python and apply them for solving real-world ML/AI problems.

chunks.title: Prerequisites and Recommended Background
chunks.content:
Prerequisites include:
• You should be comfortable with intensive Math and Programming.
• Basic knowledge about optimization (e.g., convex functions, strongly convex functions, gradient, etc.) and machine learning (e.g., supervised learning, linear regression, logistic regression, support vector machines, basic deep learning).
• Math Background: Linear algebra, Calculus, Probability and Statistics, Optimization
• Programming Background: Python, Pytorch, Tensorflow
• Preferably access to GPUs (e.g., Google Colab, TAMU HPRC)

Recommended Background:
• Proficiency in Math (linear algebra, probability/statistics, optimization). Half of homework assignments will be written questions about deriving some mathematical proofs for analyzing some machine learning problems and optimization algorithms. You should be comfortable of mathematical analysis involving functions, gradients, inequalities, matrices, vectors, random variables, expectations, etc.
• Proficiency in Python. Half of homework assignments will be programming questions. You are required to implement some optimization algorithms in Python and apply them to solve some ML/AI problems.
• Familiar with PyTorch. Some coding assignments involving deep learning will use PyTorch, which is an excellent python-based toolbox for machine learning and deep learning. Check out the official tutorial.
• Some Foundations of Machine Learning and Deep Learning. You should be familiar with some basic machine learning methods/techniques, e.g., linear regression, logistic regression, support vector machine, regularization, and have some basic knowledge of deep learning. You will need to set up a deep neural network and run some optimization algorithms for learning the parameters of the deep neural network on some large-scale datasets, and conduct the evaluations.
• Recommended Background Courses. CSCE 110 Programming, MATH 304 Linear Algebra, MATH 411 Mathematical Probability, CSCE 625/425 Artificial Intelligence, CSCE 633/421 Machine Learning, CSCE 636 Deep Learning.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
Learning outcomes assessment
chunks.content: The learning outcomes will be assessed based on homework assignments and a final course project.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This is course will be based on the book draft “Compositional Optimization for Advanced Machine learning” authored by the instructor. Its website is the following:
https://opt4ml.org/.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
• Homework assignments: 45%
• Project: 55% (Proposal 10%, Presentation 15%, Final Results 30%)

chunks.title: Grading Disputes
chunks.content: Questions on assignment grading should be discussed with the instructor. Grading dispute period for the course project will be determined and announced at the time of grade posting based on university deadline for submitting letter grades. Grading disputes for all other graded materials (other than the course project) must be presented to the respective party within ONE week upon receiving grading results (timed as email notification). All grades after the dispute time windows are considered final.

chunks.title: Late Policies and Excused Absences
chunks.content:
Late submission caused by excused absence (defined in Student rule 7) will be accepted. Excused absence will not be counted towards late days. Contact the instructor if you think you have an extremely rare circumstance for which we should make an exception. This policy is to ensure that feedback can be given in a timely manner.

Unexpected excused absences: In cases where prior notification of excused absence is not feasible (e.g., accident or emergency) the student must provide notification by the end of the second working day after the absence, including an explanation of why notice could not be sent prior to the class.

Each homework assignment due by midnight (11:59pm) on the due date. Late homeworks will not be graded so please just hand in whatever you have completed. Solution sketches will be provided after homeworks have been turned in. Late project submissions will not be accepted.

chunks.title: Collaboration
chunks.content: Both homework assignments and projects are team-based, and each student may join a team of 2 members to collaborate. Only one copy of the homework or project needs to be submitted per team, and every submission must clearly acknowledge each member's contributions. If a student is found to be uncooperative, they may be removed from the team and required to form or join a new team if necessary.

chunks.title: Free Speech and Civil Discourse
chunks.content: Texas A&M recognizes that the pursuit of truth through open and robust discourse is critical to academic inquiry. However, as a community of scholars, the university has an aspirational expectation that such discourse will be conducted in accordance with Aggie Core Values. In this "marketplace of ideas," we encourage civil dialogue creating an environment that allows individuals to express their ideas and to have their ideas challenged in respectful and responsible ways. Students can learn more about Freedom of Expression and Free Speech on the University's website about the First Amendment.

chunks.title: Artificial Intelligence Statements
chunks.content: The use of AI tools such as large language models for completing homework assignments, exams, or the course project is strictly prohibited. However, it is acceptable to use them for tasks such as editing your writing or assisting in debugging your code.
